assumptions: The developer has a supported browser and an email address.
No Resend account, API key, or verified domain exist yet.
The developer goal is to send a first transactional email after platform setup.

developer_goal: Create a Resend account, obtain sending credentials, and send the first transactional email through an official quickstart.

first_success.outcome: Unresolved across peer quickstarts; each language or CLI route returns that route's successful send response after shared account and API-key setup.
first_success.observable_signal: For SDK routes, a successful emails.send-style response with an email id; the exact client differs by quickstart.

route_selection.surface: Resend Introduction quickstart grid after Dashboard account and API key setup
route_selection.rule: When official docs present peer language and CLI quickstarts without labeling a default, do not invent a winner.

candidates.name: Node.js Quickstart
candidates.reason: Listed among many peer Quickstart cards; Introduction does not label Node.js as the default over Next.js, Python, CLI, or others.

candidates.name: Next.js Quickstart
candidates.reason: Peer framework quickstart with the same transactional-email terminal class.

candidates.name: Express, PHP, Laravel, Python, Ruby, Rails, Go, Rust, Elixir, Java, or .NET Quickstart
candidates.reason: Introduction presents these as equal Quickstart entries beside Node.js and Next.js.

candidates.name: CLI Quickstart
candidates.reason: A separate top-level quickstart reaching a send through the CLI rather than an SDK.

candidates.name: Marketing Broadcast
candidates.reason: Introduction separates marketing campaigns from transactional email; quickstarts target the first transactional send.

required_path.action: Create a Resend account from the public signup page.

required_fields.label: Terms, Acceptable Use, and Privacy Policy acknowledgement
required_fields.notes: Signup states that creating an account constitutes agreement; Google and GitHub alternatives are also offered.

required_path.observable_result: The developer has an authenticated Resend account and can open the Dashboard.

required_path.action: Create an API key from the Dashboard API keys page.
required_path.observable_result: A one-time API key secret is displayed for secure storage.

required_path.action: Choose one official quickstart implementation after shared setup.

required_fields.label: Quickstart / language or CLI choice
required_fields.notes: Introduction lists many peer quickstarts without naming a default.

required_path.observable_result: A single implementation guide becomes the measured path to first send.

prerequisites.description: Introduction states a Resend API key is required before sending.

Introduction also states a domain verified with Resend is needed to get started; several SDK quickstarts demonstrate onboarding@resend.dev for testing, which may shorten or bypass custom-domain verification for a first test send.

external_gates.description: If a custom domain is required for the selected route, DNS providers must accept Resend DKIM/SPF/MX records before verification completes.

unavoidable_waits.description: When domain verification is in-path, Resend must verify DNS before the domain is ready for production-like sending.

platform_outcomes.description: Resend issues a one-time API key secret when Create API Key succeeds.
A successful send on the selected quickstart returns that route's email id / success payload.

excluded.reason: Introduction separates marketing campaigns from transactional quickstarts.

excluded.item: AI Onboarding / MCP agent paths as the primary human route
excluded.reason: They still require a human-created account and API key, and add agent tooling after that shared gate.

excluded.item: Reading documentation as a counted action
excluded.reason: Documentation is evidence, not a product interaction.

sources.title: Introduction
evidence_supported: Peer quickstart grid across many languages plus CLI

sources.title: Add a domain
evidence_supported: Custom domain verification path referenced from Introduction

sources.title: Send emails with Node.js
evidence_supported: Successful send response shape with id

sources.title: AI Onboarding
evidence_supported: Agent and MCP routes that still presuppose human account and key creation

sources.title: Resend signup
evidence_supported: Terms, Acceptable Use, and Privacy Policy acknowledgement

uncertainties.question: Which of Resend's peer quickstarts should define the canonical first transactional-email path?
uncertainties.impact: Language and CLI choices change install commands and code actions while sharing account and API-key setup; rule 5 forbids inventing a default among equal consequential routes.
uncertainties.evidence_needed: A vendor-labeled default quickstart, or an explicit corpus decision naming one implementation after confirming no official default exists.

uncertainties.question: For the eventual selected quickstart, is custom-domain verification required before first success, or does onboarding@resend.dev (or equivalent test sender) satisfy the official terminal?
uncertainties.impact: Domain DNS steps and waits may be in-path or excludable depending on whether the selected guide's test sender is accepted as first success.
uncertainties.evidence_needed: Reconcile Introduction's domain prerequisite with the selected quickstart's demonstrated from address and success criteria.

uncertainties.question: Are email verification or other post-password screens required before Dashboard API key creation?
uncertainties.impact: Additional account actions could exist behind authentication after the observed signup form.
uncertainties.evidence_needed: A dated fresh-account walkthrough from Create account through the API keys page.
